input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a building (infrastructure asset) within the architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO) domain. It represents built environment infrastructure that spans the full building lifecycle from design through operation and refurbishment, and is characterized by the need for integrated, data‑driven management in a complex and heterogeneous infrastructure environment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides real‑time monitoring that yields real‑time insights, simulation and analysis of the building’s behavior, optimization of operational parameters, predictive analytics for forecasting future performance, and decision‑support capabilities that enhance stakeholder decision‑making.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The framework enriches BIM models with device definitions (sensors, actuators, controllers) yielding geometric models from the building geometry, behavioral models embodied in the control logic, and analytical models derived from the semantic relationships. Data sources comprise the original BIM asset information and live sensor streams. MDE techniques extract structured representations from the enriched BIM, managing them as interoperable model artifacts that preserve relationships (e.g., sensor‑actuator‑controller bindings) and are automatically transformed into deployable DT components.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators and controllers are modelled directly inside the BIM environment by the Building Control Engineer, who also defines the associated control logic. These virtual actuator definitions are synthesized into runtime components that can be invoked by the Digital Twin to affect the physical infrastructure. The framework provides a remote control interface through the real‑time data exchange channel, enabling the Facility Manager to issue actuation commands (e.g., open/close, start/stop) that are translated into the physical devices. Specific actuator types, actuation ranges, safety constraints, or addressing hierarchies are not detailed in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are modeled directly in BIM, providing a semantic description of measurement type, location and connectivity. Real‑time data exchange continuously streams sensor readings to the DT, synchronizing physical and virtual states. While the document does not enumerate specific sensor classes, sampling rates and precision are assumed to be governed by the underlying device model and the real‑time communication layer.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The framework provides real‑time data exchange capabilities that continuously synchronize physical building sensors and control systems with their digital counterparts; data representing operational parameters is streamed into the Digital Twin through the central DT Framework, which acts as a hub for ingesting this information and feeding interactive visualizations for monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Through the same central hub, the Digital Twin issues control commands back to the building control systems, enabling Facility Managers to affect physical equipment in real time; the bidirectional exchange supports monitoring‑to‑control loops where the DT’s analytics generate actions that are enacted on the physical assets.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The technical connection is realized via a modular architecture with standardized extension points and runtime configuration management, allowing heterogeneous components to interoperate; the network design emphasizes scalability, reliability, and low‑overhead updates, meeting non‑functional requirements for interoperability and maintainable communication across the DT infrastructure.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Deployment follows a model‑driven approach where enriched BIM models are automatically transformed into operational Digital Twins; the DT Framework supports automated creation, configuration, and deployment on scalable infrastructure, with runtime configuration enabling adjustments without full redeployment and ensuring continuous availability across the building lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The framework defines a full DT life‑cycle composed of (1) Device‑Modeling stage where building control engineers enrich BIM models with explicit sensor, actuator and controller entities; (2) Model‑Transformation stage that applies Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) to extract a structured semantic representation and generate executable DT artifacts; (3) Deployment stage in which the generated artifacts are instantiated on the runtime platform; (4) Operation stage characterized by continuous real‑time data exchange between the physical building and its digital counterpart and interactive, immersive visualization for monitoring and control; and (5) Evolution stage where modular extension points allow DT engineers to plug in custom services, analytics or updated control logic. Transition between stages is automated by the MDE pipeline, and governance is expressed through the same pipeline, ensuring that each artefact is traceable to its originating BIM model.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process is model‑driven: enriched BIM models are the single source of truth and are processed by a Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) transformation chain that automatically derives a formal semantic model and synthesises deployable DT components. Development is performed by three roles (Building Control Engineer, Facility Manager, DT Engineer) each contributing at defined stages. Quality assurance and testing are implicitly supported by the automated generation step, which minimizes manual coding errors; however the documents do not detail explicit testing frameworks, version‑control repositories or formal validation procedures. Evolution is handled through standardized extension points in the modular architecture, allowing DT engineers to add or replace services without redeploying the whole twin.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The twin provides decision support through real‑time data exchange and interactive visualization that expose current building states to the Facility Manager. Analytics and higher‑level insights can be delivered by custom services that are integrated via the framework’s extension points, enabling predictive control, fault detection or energy‑optimization recommendations. Recommendations are presented within the immersive interface, but the source documents do not specify confidence scores or explicit decision‑making workflows.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The solution is built on the Building Information Modeling (BIM) standard, ensuring compatibility with established AECO‑specific standards. It adopts standardized extension points within its modular architecture to promote interoperability and to allow third‑party services to be integrated without breaking compliance. By grounding the entire twin lifecycle in BIM‑derived semantics, the framework aligns with industry‑wide specifications for data exchange and model representation.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

Supported by quotes: 'architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO) domain', 'complete building life cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations', 'virtual representations of physical assets'.

The description is highly specific and well-supported by the text, listing the correct services such as real-time monitoring, simulation, analysis, optimization, predictive analytics, and decision-making. It captures the capabilities described for the Digital Twin in the AECO context. Supported by quotes: 'enabling real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization', 'providing real-time insights, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the provided text explicitly mentions specific tools and frameworks used for the Digital Twin, such as Eclipse Hono, Ditto, and Unreal Engine. Therefore, the claim is incorrect based on the evidence. Supported by quotes: 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization'.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The source documents mention 'real-time monitoring', 'real-time data exchange', and 'live synchronization' between physical assets and digital twins. Therefore, stating that the characteristic is 'Not in Document' is incorrect as relevant temporal information is present.

The provided text discusses scalable deployment and modular architecture but does not describe specific details regarding multiple digital twin instances, hierarchical structures, centralized vs decentralized architectures, or coordination mechanisms between twins. Therefore, the 'Not in Document' description is accurate.

The source documents explicitly describe the overall orchestration and architecture of the framework. It details the 'model-driven framework', the integration of components like 'Eclipse Hono and Ditto' and 'Unreal Engine', and states that the 'DT Framework acts as a central hub' for coordination. Thus, the characteristic is present in the document.

The documents describe integration with external systems and standards, specifically mentioning that the framework 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange' and addresses 'interoperability across heterogeneous systems'. Therefore, claiming it is 'Not in Document' is incorrect.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

The mention of 'analytical models' is slightly inferred from the text's discussion of 'semantic relationships' and 'optimization,' but the core description of geometric, behavioral, and data sources is well-supported.

The description accurately details the workflow involving the Building Control Engineer and Facility Manager, as well as the transformation of virtual definitions into runtime components. It correctly identifies the provision of a remote control interface via real-time exchange. It is specific to the system described, though it appropriately notes the absence of specific technical constraints (safety, ranges) in the provided text. Supported by quotes: 'Building Control Engineer... defines the control logic', 'Facility Manager... operates the physical infrastructure via the DT', and 'automatically generate deployable DT artifacts'.

This accurately describes the modeling of sensors in BIM and the real-time synchronization using Eclipse Hono and Ditto. The specific semantic attributes (measurement type, location) are reasonable inferences given BIM's nature, and the admission that specific classes are not enumerated is correct.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. Upon reviewing the provided source snippets, there is no mention of model fidelity requirements, validation/verification methods (beyond a passing reference to validating the model without description), uncertainty sources, error detection/correction, or quality assurance processes. Therefore, the assessment is correct. No relevant evidence is present.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
